title: Make quantization upsert incremental (fix O(N)-per-batch rebuild)
description: upsert currently calls rebuild_quant() which rescans all vectors for min/max and re-quantizes the whole matrix on every batch -- O(N) per batch, quadratic for incremental indexing (a stated use case). Fix: quantize only newly-appended rows against existing params; refit params geometrically (when row count outgrows the fit set by 2x) for amortized O(1) per row.
close_reason: Quantization fixed (symmetric int8), benchmarked, made incremental; PR A

title: Benchmark int8 quantization: recall vs rescore, speed vs exact
description: Add a dedicated quantization benchmark to nidus-bench that sweeps the rescore factor (1/2/4/8), measuring recall@k against independent exact ground truth and query speedup vs the f32 brute-force path. Goal: prove quantization wins and pick the default rescore from data.
close_reason: Quantization fixed (symmetric int8), benchmarked, made incremental; PR A

title: Metadata-only queries (search without a vector)
description: Allow search to accept no query vector, returning filter-matched records without scoring. Fills the gap where callers want 'give me everything where lang=rust' without fabricating a dummy vector.
close_reason: Closed

title: int8 scalar quantization
description: Store int8 quantized vectors alongside f32. Scan quantized copies for 4x less memory and faster dot, then f32-rerank top candidates. Extends exact search regime before needing ANN. Already specced as deferred seam in SPEC §9.
close_reason: Shipped: model::Quantization + store::QuantState + two-pass search_quantized (int8 first pass → f32 rerank), since extended with incremental upsert (0.6.1) and parallel int8 first pass (0.7.1). Stale claim.

title: Add distance metric selection (Cosine, Euclidean, DotProduct)
description: Currently nidus only supports cosine similarity. Add a Distance enum (Cosine, Euclidean, DotProduct) to Config, pinned at store creation in the data header (byte 12, backward-compatible). Cosine normalizes on insert; Euclidean and DotProduct store raw vectors. All scores follow 'higher = more relevant'.
close_reason: Shipped: model::Distance { Cosine, Euclidean, DotProduct } pinned in the data header, selected via Config::distance. Stale in-progress claim never closed.

title: Atomic appends + batch rollback (data & log) — prevent ENOSPC corruption
description:
DataSegment::append and OpLog::append each do a single write_all; a partial write on ENOSPC leaves a partial row / torn frame, and the next append writes past it → misaligned matrix or a mid-file torn frame that parse_all_frames rejects as hard Corruption on reopen. A process that catches the Err and keeps writing corrupts the store.

Fix:
- src/data/mod.rs::append: capture pre-write offset (stream_position); on write_all error, set_len+seek back so a torn partial row never persists (atomic per row). New truncate_to(rows) batch-rollback primitive (file set_len to HEADER+rows*stride, seek, truncate vectors to rows*dim; works file+in-memory).
- src/log/mod.rs::append: same atomic treatment (roll back partial frame on failure). New offset()->u64 and truncate_to(offset) primitives.
- src/store/mod.rs::upsert becomes a transaction: capture data_mark=row_count & log_mark=offset; reserve up-front; append vectors (rollback data on err); sync data (rollback on err); append log ops (rollback log+data on err); sync log (rollback both); THEN commit to in-RAM index (infallible, capacity pre-reserved). Other log writers (delete/drop/set_meta/create_collection) rely on per-frame atomic append.

Tests: truncate_to round-trips (file-backed, miri-ignored); log offset/truncate_to drop trailing frames and replay remainder; end-to-end batch rollback via a #[cfg(test)] fail_after seam on DataSegment forcing a mid-batch append error, asserting row_count/file len/log offset/index all restored, and reopen consistent.
acceptance_criteria: A failed append (ENOSPC mid-batch) leaves the store byte-identical to its pre-call state; reopen replays cleanly with no Corruption error; new truncate_to/offset primitives covered by file-backed tests; just ci + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented + verified: atomic appends & all-or-nothing upsert (data+log rollback), try_reserve OOM→Err across vector matrix + index maps with streaming open, max_vector_bytes cap + footprint(), and per-module SPEC.md consolidated into root SPEC.md. just ci + just miri green; cargo doc clean.

title: Graceful failure under resource exhaustion
description: nidus keeps every vector in RAM and on disk. Today, running out of room fails badly: (1) a partial write_all on ENOSPC corrupts the data matrix / op-log mid-file; (2) Vec/HashMap growth aborts the process on OOM instead of returning Err; (3) no footprint introspection or capacity cap. This epic turns exhaustion aborts into recoverable errors, makes batch writes all-or-nothing so a failure can never corrupt the store, and adds a deterministic vector-bytes cap + footprint() so hosts can refuse work proactively (the only defense reliable under cgroup/overcommit SIGKILL). Stays in-RAM brute-force; NOT spill-to-disk/mmap/ANN. Pure safe Rust, forbid(unsafe_code) intact (try_reserve is safe std).
close_reason: All child issues complete; graceful-failure epic delivered.

title: Frictionless single-box getting-started for local enthusiasts
description: nidus's audience is the local-first enthusiast who just wants easy local vector search on one machine (no cloud). The engine is fit for purpose; the gap is purely getting-started friction. Audit and reduce the path from zero to 'local search running': low-/zero-config 'nidus serve' (e.g. sensible defaults so dimension isn't a hard upfront ask where avoidable), an install path that doesn't assume a Rust toolchain, and a copy-paste quickstart that stands up a working local store in a few commands. Scope: ease/docs/ergonomics only — no engine changes, no cloud, no scale features.

title: Add range + negation filter predicates (Lt/Le/Gt/Ge, Ne/NotIn)
description: The metadata filter surface is Eq/Glob/In, AND-only — too thin for real semantic-search consumers, who routinely need numeric/date ranges (created_at > X, version >= N) and exclusions (status != archived, tag not in {...}). Add range comparisons (Lt/Le/Gt/Ge) and negation (Ne/NotIn) as new Predicate variants. Pure-logic, Miri-clean, zero deps, no FFI. Predicate derives serde and CLI(--where JSON)/server take Filter via serde, so new variants flow through lib + CLI + HTTP for free; just matching logic + Value ordering + tests + docs. Additive and backward-compatible (old filters unchanged). OR/disjunction is a separate, bigger follow-up.
design: New Predicate variants: Ne(key,Value), NotIn(key,Vec<Value>), Lt/Le/Gt/Ge(key,Value). Semantics consistent with existing predicates — a predicate is a positive assertion about a PRESENT attribute: absent key or incomparable type => no match (same as Eq/Glob/In returning false on absent). Range ordering is same-type only via a value_cmp(&Value,&Value)->Option<Ordering> helper: Int/Int numeric, Str/Str lexical, Bool/Bool (false<true); Null/List/cross-type => None => no match. Ne/NotIn match iff key present AND not-equal/not-in. Document the absent-key rule explicitly in SPEC §7.1. Alternative considered (absent satisfies Ne, SQL-ish) rejected for consistency; an explicit Exists predicate or OR support can cover it later.
acceptance_criteria: Lt/Le/Gt/Ge/Ne/NotIn match correctly for Int/Str/Bool; absent key and incomparable type => no match; cross-type => no match; List/Null never range-match. Round-trips through serde (CLI --where JSON + server Filter). Inline Miri-clean tests cover present/absent/wrong-type/boundary. SPEC §7.1 + search guide + CLI --where help updated. just ci + just ci-cli + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented: Ne/NotIn + Lt/Le/Gt/Ge predicates with same-type orderable comparison (value_cmp) and a consistent present-key rule. Pure-logic/Miri-clean, flows through CLI+server via serde. PR #12.

title: Improve parallel-scan scaling (parallelize quant path; cut serial overhead)
description:
Opt-in parallel scan (Config::query_threads) currently gives only ~1.2-1.6x at 4-8 threads. The f32 path is memory-bandwidth-bound (streams the whole matrix), so it's near its physical ceiling there. The real levers:

1. Parallelize the QUANTIZED first-pass. It's the biggest opportunity: int8 moves 4x fewer bytes, so it's compute- not bandwidth-bound and should scale with threads far better than f32. search_quantized() is currently single-threaded regardless of query_threads -- wire the same std::thread::scope chunk+merge pattern (already used for the f32 path in score_chunk/search) into the int8 first pass, then keep the f32 rerank serial (it's only top_k*rescore rows).

2. Cut serial overhead (Amdahl). The scan buffer build + sort_unstable_by_key runs single-threaded before the parallel region and caps speedup. Options: build per-thread scan buffers, or skip the global row-sort on the parallel path (the per-chunk heaps don't need a globally sorted scan -- the storage-order prefetch benefit is per-chunk).

3. Make PARALLEL_SCAN_FLOOR dim-aware. Per-row cost scales with dim, so a fixed 4096-row floor is too coarse; threshold on rows*dim (work) instead of rows.

Validate each with 'just bench-crit parallel_search' (extend it to also sweep the quantized path). Keep it pure-safe-Rust, no new deps, opt-in. Set honest expectations: f32 won't scale linearly; the quant path is where threads should pay off.
design: Reuse score_chunk + the thread::scope merge already in store::search for the int8 path. Don't add a persistent thread pool (spawn cost is microseconds vs millisecond scans -- not the bottleneck).
close_reason: Parallelized the int8 first-pass (~2.4x at 4 threads vs f32's ~1.4x plateau), skipped the global row-sort on the parallel path (per-chunk sort instead), and made the scan floor dim-aware (rows*dim work). bench-crit parallel_search extended to sweep the quant path.

title: Parallel scan for search (std threads, opt-in)
description: SPEC 9 deferred seam: fan the brute-force row scan across std::thread::scope chunks with per-chunk TopK + merge. Opt-in via Config (default single-threaded, backward-compatible). Pure Rust, no new deps.
close_reason: list pagination + opt-in parallel scan shipped in PR #10

title: Pagination for list (offset parameter)
description: list(scope, filter, limit) has no offset/cursor, so paging through all matching records isn't expressible. Add offset to the Rust API, CLI, and server.
close_reason: list pagination + opt-in parallel scan shipped in PR #10

title: justfile recipes + docs/README for CLI & server

title: CLI + HTTP server mode for nidus

Library stays pure (cargo add nidus = zero extra deps); cargo binstall nidus fetches prebuilt binaries. Includes binstall metadata, CI build/test of the feature, and a cross-target release binary matrix.

title: Add Astro/Starlight docs site deployed to GitHub Pages
description: Build a documentation site under docs/ using Astro + Starlight (mirroring the wdpkr setup), themed with the nidus nest palette (icon-4.svg), deployed to nidus.duckedup.org via a GitHub Action on push to main.
close_reason: Astro/Starlight docs site added under docs/, nest-themed, deployed via .github/workflows/docs.yml to nidus.duckedup.org

title: Proactive max_vector_bytes cap + Nidus::footprint() introspection
description:
The only exhaustion defense reliable under cgroup/overcommit (where the kernel SIGKILLs before malloc returns null, so try_reserve never fires) is to refuse work before allocating.

- src/config.rs: add pub max_vector_bytes: Option<u64> (default None, no behavior change) + builder setter. Caps the vector matrix (rows*dim*4) — the predictable host-facing number. Bounds physical rows incl. not-yet-compacted dead rows, so compact() reclaims headroom (document).
- Enforcement: store::upsert projects (row_count+records.len())*dim*4; if > cap, bail! with a capacity message BEFORE any append (clean refusal, no rollback, store stays usable for reads/search). DataSegment::open (cap passed through from Store::open): if on-disk data_bytes already exceeds cap, bail! BEFORE the big allocation — turns 'abort on open' into a clear error.
- Introspection: src/model.rs add pub struct Footprint { rows, dead_rows, dimension, vector_bytes, doc_count } (cheap, no alloc). Store::footprint(); expose Nidus::footprint() and re-export Footprint from crate root in src/lib.rs.

Tests (in-memory + file): max_vector_bytes rejects over-budget upsert with store still searchable; open with cap below file size returns clean Err (not panic) mentioning capacity; footprint() field math before/after upsert/delete/compact.
acceptance_criteria: Config::max_vector_bytes enforced on upsert (refuse before append) and open (refuse before allocation); Nidus::footprint() returns rows/dead_rows/dimension/vector_bytes/doc_count; default None preserves current behavior; just ci + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented + verified: atomic appends & all-or-nothing upsert (data+log rollback), try_reserve OOM→Err across vector matrix + index maps with streaming open, max_vector_bytes cap + footprint(), and per-module SPEC.md consolidated into root SPEC.md. just ci + just miri green; cargo doc clean.

title: try_reserve: turn OOM aborts into Result, and halve the open() peak
description:
Vec/HashMap growth aborts the process (handle_alloc_error) on allocator-null OOM instead of returning Err. Convert to anyhow::Err at every site std allows.

- Vec sites (try_reserve/try_reserve_exact, map failure to anyhow!): DataSegment::append (before extend_from_slice), compact's new_rows, rewrite's to_vec, store::search scan, upsert new_rows, get_all result.
- DataSegment::open: replace read-all-bytes-then-decode (raw Vec<u8> + decoded Vec<f32>, ~2x peak) with try_reserve_exact the final Vec<f32> once, then stream the file in bounded byte chunks converting LE->f32 into it. Single allocation, fallible, peak ≈ steady state.
- HashMap sites: try_reserve on collections and col.docs before batch insert in upsert and in the open-replay loop.
- OpLog::open: pre-try_reserve before read_to_end using file length.

std limitation (document inline, NOT a follow-up): BTreeMap and String have no try_reserve, so attrs (BTreeMap<String,Value>) clones and id Strings in upsert/replay stay abort-on-OOM — a language constraint. Small relative to the N*dim*4 matrix; the Gap-3 cap covers dominant memory. Add // NOTE: at each such site + a durability-docs line.

Tests (pure, Miri-clean): float-reserve helper with absurd n returns Err without allocating.
acceptance_criteria: Allocator-null OOM on the vector matrix + index HashMaps surfaces as anyhow::Err, not abort; DataSegment::open uses a single Vec<f32> allocation (no 2x transient); BTreeMap/String std limitation documented inline + in docs; just ci + just miri green.
close_reason: Implemented + verified: atomic appends & all-or-nothing upsert (data+log rollback), try_reserve OOM→Err across vector matrix + index maps with streaming open, max_vector_bytes cap + footprint(), and per-module SPEC.md consolidated into root SPEC.md. just ci + just miri green; cargo doc clean.

title: nidus dot() is a scalar reduction — ~1.6x slower than LanceDB SIMD at scale
description: Finding from nidus-bench (epic nidus-rq9): on exact brute-force cosine KNN, nidus is a reproducible ~1.6x slower than LanceDB at 50k-100k x 384 (nidus ~22ms vs lancedb ~13.5ms p50 @100k; stable across runs, all recall 100%). Diagnosis: NOT variance, NOT parallelism (capping RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1/TOKIO_WORKER_THREADS=1 left lancedb unchanged). Root cause: src/search/mod.rs dot() is a sequential f32 reduction (a.iter().zip(b).map(|(x,y)|x*y).sum()) which LLVM can't auto-vectorize without fast-math (FP non-associativity), so it runs scalar. LanceDB's lance-linalg uses explicit SIMD (NEON). nidus sits mid-pack: faster than DuckDB (~30ms), ~1.6x slower than LanceDB. Proposed fix (stays pure safe stable Rust, no unsafe/fast-math/deps): rewrite dot as a chunked reduction with N independent accumulators (e.g. 8 lanes) so each lane is an independent sum LLVM CAN vectorize, combined at the end. Caveat: changes summation order -> slightly different f32 rounding (may reorder exact-tie boundaries; benchmark recall tolerant). Verify with just bench all + just bench-crit before/after.
notes:
FIXED — outcome far exceeded the 1.6x parity goal: nidus is now the FASTEST engine in every benchmark cell (2-6x faster than LanceDB, 4-12x faster than DuckDB), recall 100%.

Three stacked fixes (all pure safe stable Rust, no unsafe/fast-math/deps):
1. SIMD-friendly dot (src/search/mod.rs): 8 independent accumulator lanes (chunks_exact(8) + pairwise fold + scalar tail) so each lane is its own associative reduction LLVM can auto-vectorize. Closed most of the kernel gap (dim=768 reached parity).
2. No-alloc scan (src/store/mod.rs search()): TopK now carries borrowed (&str,&str) during the scan instead of eagerly building (col.to_string(), id.clone()) per row — eliminated ~50M throwaway String allocs at 100k×384. Strings/attrs materialized only for the <=k survivors.
3. Row-sorted sequential scan (src/store/mod.rs search()): collect in-scope filter-passing rows as cheap borrowed tuples, sort_unstable_by_key(row), then walk the data matrix in storage order. The 159MB matrix dwarfs L3, so the old HashMap-iteration order was DRAM-miss-bound at every row boundary; sequential order lets the prefetcher stream it. THIS was the dominant win — criterion search/100k×768 -69.8%.

Numbers (p50, 100k vectors): 384/k10 nidus 5.44ms vs lance 12.29 vs duck 32.29; 768/k10 nidus 8.09 vs lance 24.78 vs duck 69.54; 768/k100 nidus 8.57 vs lance 53.16 vs duck 64.99.

Future (separate issue, NOT done here): the row-sorted scan allocates+sorts an n-element Vec per query; doc set is static between writes, so the sorted order could be cached and invalidated on upsert/delete. Deferred to keep this change focused.
close_reason: Exceeded the goal: nidus is now the fastest engine in every bench cell (2-6x over LanceDB), recall 100%, via SIMD-friendly dot + alloc-free + storage-order scan. Follow-up nidus-dxt filed.

title: Docs recipe: running nidus across a few boxes (no built cluster machinery)
description: An enthusiast may want to spread nidus across a couple of boxes (e.g. a few Mac Minis) for more capacity. This must stay a USER-ORCHESTRATED deployment RECIPE, not a product feature: nidus carries no coordinator, replication, rebalancing, or ops machinery (that would make it the heavyweight managed cluster it is explicitly not). Write a docs recipe: run one nidus instance per box, do query fan-out + top-k merge on the client side (sound because all instances share one embedding space). Explicitly document what is intentionally out of scope (coordinator/replication/fault-tolerance). Docs only — no code in core.

title: Consolidate per-module SPEC.md files into root SPEC.md, then remove them
description:
Keep ONE spec. Fold any content not already in the root SPEC.md into the right root section, then delete the 7 per-module spec files: src/{data,filter,glob,lock,log,search,store}/SPEC.md. Repoint the module doc-comments that say 'Contract: see SPEC.md in this directory/here' to the root SPEC.md — these are in src/{lock,search,log,filter,glob,data,store}/mod.rs. (config.rs/lib.rs/model.rs already reference root SPEC.md by section — leave those.)

Do LAST, after the graceful-failure code (nidus-cw1/qzv/c3c) lands, so the new durability/limits/footprint docs are written directly into root SPEC.md and this issue just sweeps up the remaining per-module content + references.
acceptance_criteria: No src/*/SPEC.md files remain; the only spec is root SPEC.md; no doc-comment references a per-directory SPEC.md; cargo doc builds clean; just ci green.
close_reason: Implemented + verified: atomic appends & all-or-nothing upsert (data+log rollback), try_reserve OOM→Err across vector matrix + index maps with streaming open, max_vector_bytes cap + footprint(), and per-module SPEC.md consolidated into root SPEC.md. just ci + just miri green; cargo doc clean.

title: Cache the row-sorted scan order across queries
description: search() now collects in-scope rows into a Vec and sort_unstable_by_key(row) on EVERY query to get sequential data-matrix access (the big win in nidus-33k). The doc set only changes on upsert/delete, so this sorted order is recomputed redundantly across the many queries between writes. Optimization: maintain a row-sorted index (or lazily cache + invalidate on write) so steady-state search skips the per-query O(n) alloc + O(n log n) sort. Must stay correct under multi-collection scope, filters, dead rows (append-only overwrite), and Miri-clean. Measure with just bench-crit against the nidus-33k-final baseline; only worth it if it beats the already-strong current numbers without complicating the write path. Filed from nidus-33k.
notes:
Measured 2026-06-05 while doing nidus-8gx (which changed the sort landscape).

FINDINGS:
- sort_unstable_by_key on the scan is ~2.16 ms standalone at n=100k, dim=768
  (micro-bench: clone+sort and sort-only both ~2.15 ms). That's ~29% of the
  7.45 ms serial f32 query, and it scales with n. So caching it is a real win
  for LARGE-n SINGLE-query SERIAL search.
- The sort itself is ESSENTIAL and stays: skipping it (random HashMap-order
  access) blows the serial query from 7.45 ms to 22 ms (~3x slower) because
  sequential data-matrix access is the cache-friendliness win from nidus-33k.
  dxt must KEEP sorted access and only remove the per-query RECOMPUTE.

POST-8gx SCOPE NARROWED:
- 8gx made the parallel path sort PER-CHUNK inside workers (global sort skipped
  there). So dxt now only helps the SERIAL path directly. A cached global order
  could let the parallel path skip per-chunk sorts too (composes well), but the
  at-scale recommendation is now query_threads / quant, not serial.

WRITE-PATH / SYNC TENSION (the issue's gating concern):
- Store is used as Arc<RwLock<Nidus>>, so the cache cannot use RefCell (not
  Sync). Clean options: (a) lazy global cache behind RwLock<Option<Vec>> +
  dirty flag (write path just flips the flag; ~2% extra RAM for duplicated
  id/col strings; one rebuild after each write batch); (b) per-collection
  append-order index exploiting monotonic rows (no RwLock, but overwrites/
  deletes need validation-or-fallback + multi-collection needs k-way merge);
  (c) row-major doc table refactor (eliminates the sort for ALL paths, cleanest
  end state, but large higher-risk refactor across every read/write path).

DECISION 2026-06-05: deferred by user. Benefit is real but narrow post-8gx and
every clean capture touches RAM or the write path. Revisit if a consumer hits
large-n serial search as the hot path. If picked up, (a) is the recommended
low-risk start; (c) is the right long-term shape on its own branch.

title: just recipes + benchmarks README + gitignore
description: Add just recipes: parametrized 'bench ENGINES=nidus *ARGS' (case maps all->duckdb,lancedb), 'bench-crit'. Keep separate from core test/ci/lint. benchmarks/README.md with run commands + one-time heavy-build note. Ensure .gitignore covers target/bench-results/.
close_reason: LanceDB adapter (bypass_vector_index exact KNN), criterion regression suite, and just recipes + README all implemented and verified (100% parity agreement, all gates green).

title: Criterion regression suite for nidus
description: benchmarks/benches/nidus_regression.rs. criterion as dev-dep of bench crate only (never enters nidus build/test/CI). Bench nidus public API (open_in_memory -> upsert N -> search) at a few sizes for baseline-vs-baseline regression detection.
close_reason: LanceDB adapter (bypass_vector_index exact KNN), criterion regression suite, and just recipes + README all implemented and verified (100% parity agreement, all gates green).
